summary: Gruber compiles a collection of pundit predictions from 2008-2009 insisting Apple needed to enter the netbook market or face dire consequences. He contrasts these predictions against Apple's actual Q4 2009 results: 2.3 million laptops sold at an average price of $1,265 and $1.67 billion in profit — with zero netbooks. The post is a classic 'claim chowder' exercise, letting the pundits' own words serve as their indictment. The underlying argument is that Apple's refusal to chase the low-margin netbook trend was strategically correct, despite near-universal media criticism.
key_points: Apple sold 2.3 million laptops in Q4 2009 at premium prices while making zero netbooks, directly contradicting pundit predictions
Multiple tech publications from Forbes to Wired to PC World insisted Apple was making a critical mistake by ignoring the netbook market
The predictions ranged from calling Apple 'oblivious' to suggesting it should shut down during recessions if it wouldn't make cheap products
Apple's $1.67 billion quarterly profit demonstrated that refusing to compete on price in a low-margin segment was the right strategy
money_quotes: Not one of those 2.3 million laptops was a "netbook".
Amazingly, ten months later, they remain equally oblivious.
So Apple will be forced to enter the "netbook" market due to slumping MacBook sales, eh?
That would be the back-to-school period which just ended, with Apple selling 2.3 million laptops at an average price of $1,265.
Imagine how much money Apple could have lost if only it had a netbook.
key_insight: The tech press's unanimous insistence that Apple needed a netbook was proven spectacularly wrong by Apple's record laptop sales and profits, vindicating the company's refusal to chase low-margin market trends.
